chapter_summary: The closing portion of the Gheranda Samhita's first upadesha (instruction) on the shatkarma — the six purification practices of ghatastha-yoga (yoga of the body-vessel) — taught by sage Gheranda to his disciple Chanda(kapali). It completes the description of basti (yogic enema, in jala/water and shushka/dry forms, done in utkatasana with ashwini-mudra to draw and expel, curing constipation, indigestion, urinary disorders, gudavarta and disorders of krura-vayu), neti (threading a fine thread through the nostril and out the mouth, granting khechari-siddhi, divya-drishti/divine sight, and destroying kapha-dosha), lauliki/nauli (churning the belly rapidly side to side to destroy all disease and kindle the body's fire), trataka (steady unblinking gaze at a subtle point until tears flow, yielding the shambhavi state and divya-drishti), and kapalabhati in its three modes — vamakrama, vyutkrama, and shitkrama (breath and water drawn through ida/pingala and nostrils/mouth to destroy kapha-dosha, ward off old age and fever, and make the body free and luminous "like Kamadeva"). It closes the first upadesha (the colophon naming shatkarma-sadhana) and opens the second upadesha on asana: Shiva long ago declared 84 lakh asanas matching the 84 lakh species of living beings, of which 84 are distinguished, 32 are auspicious and siddhi-giving in the mortal world; these 32 are enumerated (siddha, padma, bhadra, mukta, vajra, svastika, simha, gomukha, vira, dhanura, mrita, gupta, matsya, matsyendra, goraksha, paschimottana, utkata, sankata, mayura, kukkuta, kurma, uttanakurmaka, uttanamanduka, vriksha, manduka, garuda, vrisha, shalabha, makara, ushtra, bhujanga, yoga). The fragment ends mid-description of siddhasana (pressing the perineum, gazing between the brows with restrained senses).

story.title: Gheranda Purifies the Vessel: From the Six Cleansings to the Seat of the Adept
story.arc: Sage Gheranda, answering his disciple Chanda's wish to know the yoga that liberates, finishes describing the six purifications of ghatastha-yoga — the yoga of the body treated as a clay vessel that must be fired clean before it can hold the divine. He details basti (cleansing the lower gate through water and air drawn by ashwini-mudra), neti (threading the nasal passage to open divine sight), lauliki (churning the belly to wake its inner fire), trataka (the unblinking gaze that melts into the shambhavi state), and kapalabhati in three modes that scour away phlegm, age, and fever until the body becomes 'free-willed' and radiant 'like Kamadeva.' With the first instruction sealed by its colophon, Gheranda turns at once to the second teaching: invoking Shiva's ancient word that the 84 lakh asanas mirror the 84 lakh living forms, he narrows them to 32 perfected seats fit for mortals, names each one, and begins the first — siddhasana, the adept's seat, pressing the root and gazing between the brows — leaving the disciple poised at the threshold of stillness.

teachings.teaching: The body is a vessel (ghata) that must first be purified through the six shatkarma before any higher yoga can succeed; cleansing the gross channels removes the doshas (kapha, ama, krura-vayu) that obstruct the inner fire.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya framework the shatkarma are not hygiene but pranic preparation: the nadis must be physically scoured so that prana ceases to leak into disease and can instead be gathered and turned inward. Purifying the 'vessel' is the literal precondition for the prana to rise — Kriya begins only in a body where the channels no longer dissipate force into koshtha-dosha and ama-vata.

teachings.teaching: Basti, done in water and on land with ashwini-mudra (contracting and releasing the anus), cures constipation, urinary disorders and disorders of krura-vayu, making the body free-willed (svacchanda-deha) and like Kamadeva.
teachings.lens_note: The ashwini contraction at the lowest gate is mula-territory — the apana-vayu that normally flows downward and outward is reversed and lifted. For Sharma this downward-to-upward turning of apana is the seed gesture of all Kriya: the same root-lock that here merely cleanses the bowel is, refined, the lever that unites apana with prana at the navel and begins the upward kundalini movement.

teachings.teaching: Neti — drawing a fine thread through the nostril and out the mouth — perfects khechari-siddhi, destroys kapha-dosha, and gives divine sight (divya-drishti).
teachings.lens_note: That a nasal cleansing is said to yield khechari and divine sight signals, in Sharma's reading, that the practice opens the upper passage behind the palate — the gateway khechari-mudra later inhabits. The 'divine sight' is not metaphor but the awakening of the ajna region; the gross thread prepares the subtle path the tongue and gaze will later travel inward.

teachings.teaching: Lauliki/nauli — churning the belly rapidly to both sides — destroys all diseases and increases the body's fire (dehanala).
teachings.lens_note: Churning the navel directly stokes the agni at the manipura/navel center. In Sharma's Kriya the navel is where apana and prana are made to meet and ignite; lauliki is the gross mechanical rehearsal of that union, kindling the jatharagni whose subtler form is the inner heat that drives prana up the sushumna.

teachings.teaching: Trataka — gazing without blinking at a subtle point until tears fall — surely produces the shambhavi state, prevents eye disease and bestows divine sight.
teachings.lens_note: Trataka is, for Sharma, the explicit bridge from a gross cleansing kriya to dhyana: the fixed outer gaze collapses into shambhavi-mudra, the eyes turned to the point between the brows while awareness rests inward. It is where shatkarma quietly becomes meditation — the same one-pointedness that the gaze trains externally is what holds attention at the ajna in Kriya practice.

teachings.teaching: Kapalabhati in three modes — vamakrama, vyutkrama, shitkrama — moves breath and water through ida and pingala and the nostrils and mouth to destroy kapha-dosha, banish old age and fever, and make the body radiant like Kamadeva.
teachings.lens_note: Working alternately through ida (candra, the lunar/left channel) and pingala (the solar/right channel) is overt nadi-balancing: in Sharma's framework prana and apana, sun and moon, must be equalized in the side channels before either can enter sushumna. Kapalabhati's deliberate alternation of the two is the cleansing that precedes their merger — and the promised freedom from age and fever is the early sign of prana no longer leaking outward into the body's decay.

teachings.teaching: Shiva long ago declared 84 lakh asanas equal to the 84 lakh species of living beings; of these 84 are special, 32 are auspicious and confer siddhi in the mortal world — beginning with siddhasana, in which one presses the perineum, restrains the senses, and gazes between the brows.
teachings.lens_note: That the asanas number exactly the species of life encodes, for Sharma, that posture is the means of moving through and transcending every embodied form — the seat fixes the body so consciousness can climb beyond the form it sits in. Siddhasana being named first and described with perineal pressure plus the inter-brow gaze is no accident: it locks the lower gate (apana) and fixes attention at the ajna simultaneously — the complete static frame in which Kriya pranayama is performed. The 'siddha's seat' is literally the posture of the accomplished one because it is the one that makes the inner ascent possible.
